body:
## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
Import itself is market table stakes, but an import wizard that makes structure conversion trustworthy (editable chapter/scene mapping + explicit warning summary) is a meaningful differentiator. This helps us win migrations from generic editors without copying competitors feature-for-feature.

## Competitor evidence
- Living Writer explicitly markets migration from Scrivener, Google Docs, and Word, and documents project import workflows: https://livingwriter.com/blog/migrating-to-livingwriter-from-scrivener-google-docs-word-etc and https://docs.livingwriter.com/
- Campfire help content is centered on Campfire Pro/backup-style import paths, with less emphasis on manuscript-first `.docx/.txt` onboarding wizard UX: https://help.campfirewriting.com/article/139-importing-from-campfire-pro
- Inkitt writer guidance emphasizes chapter/episode publishing flow; structured manuscript migration is not a core positioning pillar: https://writer.inkitt.com

## Risks / trade-offs
- Parser edge cases (custom Word styles, inconsistent headings) can create mapping errors if confidence is not surfaced clearly.
- More wizard steps can increase time-to-first-edit if the flow feels heavy.
- Support burden rises unless warnings are actionable and retry paths are obvious.

## Recommendation
Approve, with differentiation guardrails: require a pre-import structure preview and clear confidence/warning states so users can fix mapping before commit. Position this as "safe migration" rather than raw file ingestion.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design

## Update

Requesting Market Research approval/filter pass before engineering intake.

- Source goal: [Write a story writing app](/BIN/issues/BIN-68)
- Review ask: approve/reject/reframe with competitor-backed differentiation (Living Writer/Campfire/Inkitt)
- Next: if approved, we will route to planning/design in priority order
